---
title: "Interaction Effects"
subtitle: "When one relationship depends on another"
author: "Merlin Schaeffer · Department of Sociology"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, theme/ku.scss]
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    slide-number: c/t
    footer: "Lecture 11 · Interaction effects"
    incremental: false
    highlight-style: github
    logo: img/ku_seal.png
    code-line-numbers: true
    fig-align: center
    auto-stretch: false
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-color: "#901A1E"
  data-background-image: img/UCPH_white.png
  data-background-size: 18%
  data-background-position: 95% 5%
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
library(RefManageR)
library(knitr)
library(ggrepel)

options(htmltools.preserve.raw = FALSE,
        htmltools.dir.version = FALSE, servr.interval = 0.5, width = 115, digits = 3)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE, message = FALSE, fig.retina = 3, error = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE, cache = FALSE, fig.align = 'center',
  comment = "#", strip.white = TRUE, tidy = FALSE)

BibOptions(check.entries = FALSE,
           bib.style = "authoryear",
           style = "markdown",
           hyperlink = FALSE,
           no.print.fields = c("doi", "url", "ISSN", "urldate", "language", "note", "isbn", "volume"))
myBib <- ReadBib("../Stats_II.bib", check = FALSE)

# Helper: draw a regression plane (or warped surface) with base persp()
plane <- function(model, xvar, yvar, data, xlab, ylab, zlab, theta = 50, phi = 12) {
  xs <- seq(min(data[[xvar]]), max(data[[xvar]]), length.out = 25)
  ys <- seq(min(data[[yvar]]), max(data[[yvar]]), length.out = 25)
  grid <- expand.grid(x = xs, y = ys)
  names(grid) <- c(xvar, yvar)
  z <- matrix(predict(model, newdata = grid), nrow = 25)
  persp(xs, ys, z, theta = theta, phi = phi, col = "#901A1E33", border = "#901A1E",
        xlab = xlab, ylab = ylab, zlab = zlab, ticktype = "detailed",
        shade = 0.3, expand = 0.6, cex.axis = 0.7, cex.lab = 0.9)
}
```

## By the end of today you can … {.inverse background-color="#901A1E"}

1. recognise a **conditional relationship** — when the association between $Y$ and one predictor *depends on* another — and specify it as an **interaction**;

2. interpret the **conditional main terms** and the **interaction term**, and **mean-centre** so the main terms mean something;

3. **visualise** an interaction and read the conditional slopes straight off the picture.

::: {.backgrnote}
One part per goal. Two running questions: does education reduce **xenophobia** the same way in Denmark and Bulgaria — and do **state ownership** and **civil liberties** interact in fighting poverty?
:::

::: {.notes}
Everything so far has been *additive*: each predictor adds its own fixed amount, the same for everyone. Today that assumption breaks. Set the hook plainly — sometimes a relationship is not one number but a family of numbers, one per context. The word to keep repeating is "depends on": the effect of X *depends on* Z. That phrase is the whole lecture.
:::

## Additive, so far {.inverse background-color="#901A1E"}

::: {.push-left}
Everything we have built assumes predictors act **additively** — each adds its slice, independently of the others:

$$Y_i = \alpha + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + e_i$$

::: {.content-box-red}
**Beware:** this quietly assumes the effect of $X_1$ is **the same** at every value of $X_2$. Often it is not.
:::
:::

::: {.push-right}
::: {.content-box-blue}
**Discuss:** does more education reduce xenophobia by the *same* amount in a long-established democracy and in a post-socialist state? Should we even expect it to?
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Make the hidden assumption visible, because students never notice it until you name it. A multiple regression coefficient is a single number, so it claims the effect of education is identical for a Dane and a Bulgarian, for a man and a woman, at every age. Ask whether that is plausible for the education example — most will say no, which is exactly the door into interactions.
:::

## Does education fight xenophobia everywhere? {.inverse background-color="#901A1E"}

[Part 1 of 2]{.part-pill}

::: {.lead}
Education is the classic antidote to prejudice — in the *West*. Does the same hold in a very different context?
:::

## The research question of the day {.inverse background-color="#901A1E"}

::: {.push-left}
Both **Denmark** and **Bulgaria** have seen fierce debates over immigration. In both, we can ask whether the more educated hold **warmer** views of immigrants.

::: {.content-box-blue}
**Research question:** is the link between **education** and **xenophobia** the *same* in Denmark and Bulgaria — or does it **depend on the country**?
:::
:::

::: {.push-right}
::: {.content-box-green}
Our outcome: agreement that *"immigrants make Denmark/Bulgaria a worse place to live"* — reversed so **higher = more xenophobic**, on a 0–10 scale.
:::

::: {.backgrnote}
Data: European Social Survey round 9 (2018), Danish and Bulgarian respondents.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Frame the two countries as a genuine contrast, not a throwaway: Denmark is a wealthy, long-stable democracy; Bulgaria is post-socialist, poorer, with a different history of migration. If the education effect is really about exposure and liberal values, it might travel — or it might not. That "might not" is the interaction we are chasing.
:::

## Preparation

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Packages
```{r libs}
pacman::p_load(
  tidyverse,    # Data manipulation and visualization
  haven,        # Read SPSS files & handle labelled data
  estimatr,     # OLS with robust / weighted standard errors
  modelsummary  # Regression tables
)
```

### Get & prepare the data
```{r ess-data}
ESS <- read_spss("../assets/ESS9e03_1.sav") %>%
  filter(cntry == "DK" | cntry == "BG") %>%        # Danes and Bulgarians
  mutate(
    cntry  = as_factor(cntry) %>% fct_relevel("Denmark"),  # Denmark = reference
    gndr   = as_factor(gndr),
    eduyrs = case_when(                             # Sensible bounds
      eduyrs < 9  ~ 9,
      eduyrs > 21 ~ 21,
      TRUE        ~ zap_labels(eduyrs)
    ),
    agea    = zap_labels(agea),
    imwbcnt = 10 - zap_labels(imwbcnt)              # Reverse: higher = more xenophobic
  ) %>%
  select(idno, cntry, pspwght, gndr, eduyrs, agea, imwbcnt) %>%
  drop_na()
```

:::

::: {.notes}
Keep this quick — the only non-obvious step is reversing the outcome so that higher means *more* xenophobic, which makes a negative education slope read as "education reduces prejudice". Note we keep both countries in one data set; the whole point is to compare the education slope *across* them.
:::

## Additive multiple OLS: one slope for everyone

::: {.panel-tabset}

### The models
```{r add-mods, results = 'hide'}
ols_bi   <- lm_robust(imwbcnt ~ cntry, data = ESS, weights = pspwght)
ols_add  <- lm_robust(imwbcnt ~ cntry + eduyrs, data = ESS, weights = pspwght)

modelsummary(
  list("Country only" = ols_bi, "+ education" = ols_add),
  coef_rename = c("cntryBulgaria" = "Bulgaria", "eduyrs" = "Years of education"),
  stars = TRUE, gof_map = c("nobs", "r.squared"), output = "kableExtra"
)
```

### The table
```{r add-tab, ref.label = "add-mods", echo = FALSE, results = 'asis'}
```

::: {.content-box-green}
Bulgarians are markedly **more** xenophobic on average, and each year of education goes with **`r round(abs(coef(lm_robust(imwbcnt ~ cntry + eduyrs, data = ESS, weights = pspwght))["eduyrs"]), 3)`** points *less* xenophobia. But this forces **one education slope on both countries**.

:::

### The additive plane
```{r add-plane, echo = FALSE, out.width='52%', fig.height = 5, fig.width = 5.5}
m <- lm(imwbcnt ~ eduyrs + agea, data = ESS, weights = pspwght)
plane(m, "eduyrs", "agea", ESS,
      xlab = "Education", ylab = "Age", zlab = "Xenophobia")
```

::: {.backgrnote .center}
Additive = a **flat, tilted plane**: the education slope is the same at every age (and every country).
:::

:::

::: {.notes}
Two things. First, the additive model already tells a story — Bulgarians more xenophobic, education associated with less. Second, and this is the setup for everything: this model *forces a single education slope on both countries*. Point at the plane and say it is flat and rigid — tilt it however you like, the slope in one direction never changes as you move along the other. An interaction is what lets the plane bend.
:::

## But what if the relationship is conditional?

::: {.push-left}
::: {.content-box-blue}
Two predictors **interact** when the association between $Y$ and one of them **depends on the value of the other**.
:::

Look at the two clouds separately:

- In **Denmark**, education slopes clearly **downward** — the educated are less xenophobic.
- In **Bulgaria**, the line is nearly **flat** — education barely moves xenophobia.

$\rightarrow$ The education effect **depends on the country**. That is an interaction.
:::

::: {.push-right}
```{r cond-plot, echo = FALSE, out.width='94%', fig.height = 4.2, fig.width = 6}
ggplot(ESS, aes(y = imwbcnt, x = eduyrs, color = cntry)) +
  geom_jitter(aes(size = pspwght), alpha = 1/10) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Denmark" = "#901A1E", "Bulgaria" = "#425570")) +
  labs(y = "Xenophobia (0-10)", x = "Years of education", color = NULL) +
  guides(size = "none") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) + theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```
:::

::: {.notes}
Let them read the picture before you give them the definition — the two slopes are visibly different, and that *is* the interaction, made concrete. Say the definition twice, slowly: an interaction is when the effect of one variable depends on the level of another. Then note it is symmetric, which trips people up: "education depends on country" and "the country gap depends on education" are the same statement seen from two sides.
:::

## Specify it as multiplicative

::: {.push-left}
We add the **product** of the two predictors:

$$Y_i = \alpha + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \color{#901A1E}{\beta_3 (X_1 \times X_2)} + e_i$$

```{r inter-mod, results = 'hide'}
# The * asks R for both main terms AND their product
ols_int <- lm_robust(imwbcnt ~ cntry * eduyrs,
                     data = ESS, weights = pspwght)
```

::: {.backgrnote}
`cntry * eduyrs` is shorthand: R adds `cntry`, `eduyrs`, **and** `cntry:eduyrs`, the interaction term.
:::
:::

::: {.push-right}
::: {.small}
```{r inter-tab, echo = FALSE, results = 'asis'}
modelsummary(
  list("Additive" = ols_add, "Interaction" = ols_int),
  coef_rename = c("cntryBulgaria" = "Bulgaria", "eduyrs" = "Education",
                  "cntryBulgaria:eduyrs" = "Bulgaria × Education"),
  stars = TRUE, gof_map = c("nobs", "r.squared"), output = "kableExtra"
)
```
:::

::: {.content-box-green}
OLS handles a *multiplicative* idea by *adding one column* — the product of the two variables. The conditional relationship is **linearised**.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
The mechanics are trivial and worth saying so: an interaction is just one more predictor, the two variables multiplied together. `*` is R's shorthand that also remembers to include the main terms. The deeper point is the green box — regression is fundamentally additive, and it accommodates a "depends on" relationship by literally adding a product column. Do not interpret the coefficients yet; that is the next, harder slide.
:::

## The trap: what do the main terms mean now?

::: {.push-left}
```{r trap-tab, echo = FALSE, results = 'asis'}
modelsummary(
  list("Interaction" = ols_int),
  coef_rename = c("cntryBulgaria" = "Bulgaria", "eduyrs" = "Education",
                  "cntryBulgaria:eduyrs" = "Bulgaria × Education"),
  stars = TRUE, gof_map = c("nobs"), output = "kableExtra"
)
```
:::

::: {.push-right}
Once you add the product, each **main term is conditional** — it holds only where the *other* variable is **zero**:

$$\beta_{\text{Education}} \;\big|\; \text{Bulgaria} = 0 \quad(\text{i.e. in Denmark})$$
$$\beta_{\text{Bulgaria}} \;\big|\; \text{Education} = 0$$

::: {.content-box-red}
**Beware:** the "Bulgaria" coefficient is now the country gap **at zero years of education** — a person who does not exist. No wonder it looks strange.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This is where students fall off, so slow right down. The moment you add the product, the two main terms stop being averages and become *conditional* — each is the effect of one variable at the point where the other equals zero. The education coefficient is the Danish slope, because Bulgaria = 0 means Denmark. The country coefficient is the gap at zero years of education — nobody in the sample. That absurdity is not a bug; it is a signal that we should move zero somewhere meaningful.
:::

## The fix: mean-centre

::: {.push-left}
Shift education so that **zero = the average**:

```{r center}
ESS <- ESS %>%
  mutate(mc_eduyrs = eduyrs - mean(eduyrs))

ols_intc <- lm_robust(imwbcnt ~ cntry * mc_eduyrs,
                      data = ESS, weights = pspwght)
```

::: {.content-box-green}
Now `Education = 0` means *"a person with average schooling"* — a real, typical case. The interaction term is **unchanged**; only the main terms move to where we can read them.
:::
:::

::: {.push-right}
::: {.small}
```{r center-tab, echo = FALSE, results = 'asis'}
modelsummary(
  list("Raw" = ols_int, "Mean-centred" = ols_intc),
  coef_rename = c("cntryBulgaria" = "Bulgaria",
                  "eduyrs" = "Education", "mc_eduyrs" = "Education (centred)",
                  "cntryBulgaria:eduyrs" = "Bulgaria × Educ",
                  "cntryBulgaria:mc_eduyrs" = "Bulgaria × Educ"),
  stars = TRUE, gof_map = c("nobs"), output = "kableExtra"
)
```
:::

::: {.backgrnote .center}
The Bulgaria gap becomes the gap **at average education** — sensible, and significant.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
The cure is one line, and the effect is dramatic — watch the country coefficient move from a strange negative number to the substantively meaningful gap at average education. Stress what does *not* change: the interaction term is identical, because centring only relocates zero, it does not alter how the slopes differ. Tell them to centre continuous variables involved in interactions by default; it is almost always the right move.
:::

## Reading the interaction term

::: {.push-left}
```{r read-plot, echo = FALSE, out.width='96%', fig.height = 4.2, fig.width = 6}
ggplot(ESS, aes(y = imwbcnt, x = mc_eduyrs, color = cntry)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, colour = "grey60", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Denmark" = "#901A1E", "Bulgaria" = "#425570")) +
  labs(y = "Xenophobia (0-10)", x = "Education (centred)", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) + theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```
:::

::: {.push-right}
```{r read-nums, include = FALSE}
b <- coef(ols_intc)
dk <- b["mc_eduyrs"]; bg <- b["mc_eduyrs"] + b["cntryBulgaria:mc_eduyrs"]
```

The interaction term (**`r round(b["cntryBulgaria:mc_eduyrs"], 3)`**) reads **two** ways:

1. It is how much the **education slope changes** from Denmark to Bulgaria:
$$\underbrace{`r round(dk, 3)`}_{\text{DK slope}} + \underbrace{`r round(b["cntryBulgaria:mc_eduyrs"], 3)`}_{\text{interaction}} = \underbrace{`r round(bg, 3)`}_{\text{BG slope}}$$

2. It is how much the **country gap changes** with each year of education.

::: {.content-box-green}
Education fights xenophobia in **Denmark** (slope `r round(dk, 3)`) but is essentially **flat in Bulgaria** (slope `r round(bg, 3)`).
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Return to the symmetry, now with numbers. The single interaction coefficient answers two questions at once: how the education slope differs between countries, and how the country gap grows with education. Walk the little sum — the Danish slope plus the interaction equals the Bulgarian slope. Then the substantive headline: the liberal-education story holds in Denmark and essentially fails in Bulgaria. That is a finding, and it is exactly what a single additive slope would have hidden.
:::

## A second example: education × gender

::: {.panel-tabset}

### The picture
```{r eg-plot, echo = FALSE, out.width='58%', fig.height = 4, fig.width = 7}
ggplot(ESS, aes(y = imwbcnt, x = mc_eduyrs, color = gndr)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", fullrange = TRUE) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, colour = "grey60", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Male" = "#901A1E", "Female" = "#425570")) +
  labs(y = "Xenophobia (0-10)", x = "Education (centred)", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) + theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

### The model
```{r eg-mod, results = 'hide'}
ols_eg <- lm_robust(imwbcnt ~ mc_eduyrs * gndr,
                    data = ESS, weights = pspwght)

modelsummary(list("Xenophobia" = ols_eg),
             coef_rename = c("mc_eduyrs" = "Education (centred)",
                             "gndrFemale" = "Female",
                             "mc_eduyrs:gndrFemale" = "Education × Female"),
             stars = TRUE, gof_map = c("nobs"), output = "kableExtra")
```

### Read it
```{r eg-tab, ref.label = "eg-mod", echo = FALSE, results = 'asis'}
```

::: {.content-box-blue}
**Discuss:** interpret the **Female** coefficient. At what value of education does it hold — and is that why it may look small?
:::

:::

::: {.notes}
A second worked case cements the pattern, now with two continuous-looking main terms plus their product. Push them to state the conditions out loud: the education slope is "among men", the gender gap is "at average education". Whether the slopes differ significantly matters less here than that they can *say what each number is conditional on*. That is the transferable skill.
:::

## Break {.inverse background-color="#901A1E"}

<div class="ku-timer" data-min="15"></div>

## Your turn: exercise 1

::: {.left-column}
You test whether the **Bali terror-attack effect** was the same across **ten** countries — a treatment × country interaction on Legewie's data.

[**Open exercise 1 in a new tab ↗**](11-exercise1.html){target="_blank"}

<div class="ku-timer" data-min="20"></div>
:::

::: {.right-column}
<iframe src='11-exercise1.html' width='100%' height='620' frameborder='0' scrolling='yes' style="border:1px solid #ddd; border-radius:6px;"></iframe>
:::

## Two continuous predictors {.inverse background-color="#901A1E"}

[Part 2 of 2]{.part-pill}

::: {.lead}
Back to the state-ownership triangle from Lectures 2 and 9 — but now the two arms of the triangle **interact**.
:::

## Revised research question {.inverse background-color="#901A1E"}

::: {.push-left}
In **Lecture 9** we asked whether **civil liberties** confound or mediate the link between **state ownership of the economy** and **poverty**.

Today we ask something sharper:

::: {.content-box-blue}
**Research question:** does state ownership fight poverty **differently depending on the level of civil liberties** — strong under one, weak under the other?
:::
:::

::: {.push-right}
```{tikz tri, echo = FALSE, out.width='84%'}
\usetikzlibrary{shapes.geometric, arrows.meta, positioning}
\definecolor{kured}{HTML}{901A1E}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, semithick]
\sffamily
\node[ellipse, draw, align=center] (S) at (0,0) {State\\ownership};
\node[ellipse, draw, align=center] (F) at (5,0) {Civil\\liberties};
\node[ellipse, draw] (P) at (2.5,-2.6) {Poverty};
\draw[->, kured, very thick] (S) -- (P);
\draw[->, kured, very thick] (F) -- (P);
\draw[<->, kured, thick, dashed] (S) -- node[above]{interact?} (F);
\end{tikzpicture}
```
:::

::: {.notes}
Tie this explicitly to Lecture 9 so it reads as one continuing story, not a new topic. There we treated civil liberties as a possible confounder or mediator; here we ask whether the two predictors *interact* — whether state ownership's effect on poverty is one thing under liberal rule and another under autocracy. Same three variables, a new and more demanding question.
:::

## Build the triangle data

```{r vdem-data}
pacman::p_load(tidyverse, estimatr, vdemdata, wbstats, modelsummary)

# V-Dem: civil liberties, and state ownership (reversed so higher = more)
Dat_vdem <- vdem %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  select(country_text_id, year,
         civ_liberties = v2xcl_rol,
         state_own_raw = v2clstown) %>%
  mutate(state_ownership = -state_own_raw)

# World Bank: extreme poverty (< $3.00 a day). Live, with cached fallback.
Dat_pov <- tryCatch(
  wb_data("SI.POV.DDAY", start_date = 1972, end_date = 2025),
  error = function(e) readRDS("data/wb_poverty_raw.rds")
) %>%
  rename(poverty = SI.POV.DDAY, year = date, country_text_id = iso3c) %>%
  select(country_text_id, year, country, poverty) %>%
  drop_na(poverty) %>%
  group_by(country) %>% filter(year == max(year)) %>% ungroup()

Dat <- inner_join(Dat_pov, Dat_vdem, by = c("country_text_id", "year")) %>%
  drop_na(poverty, state_ownership, civ_liberties) %>%
  mutate(                                # Mean-centre BOTH, ready for the interaction
    mc_state = state_ownership - mean(state_ownership),
    mc_civ   = civ_liberties  - mean(civ_liberties)
  )
```

::: {.notes}
This is the exact L2/L9 pipeline — live World Bank data with a cached fallback so the render never dies on an API outage, the reversed V-Dem state-ownership measure, and the $3.00 poverty line. The new step is at the bottom: mean-centre *both* continuous predictors before interacting them, for the same reason as in Part 1 — so the main terms read at the average, not at an impossible zero.
:::

## Additive first: one flat plane

::: {.push-left}
```{r p2-add, results = 'hide'}
ols_add2 <- lm_robust(poverty ~ mc_state + mc_civ,
                      data = Dat)

modelsummary(list("Poverty" = ols_add2),
             coef_rename = c("mc_state" = "State ownership",
                             "mc_civ" = "Civil liberties"),
             stars = TRUE, gof_map = c("nobs", "r.squared"),
             output = "kableExtra")
```

::: {.content-box-green}
The additive state-ownership slope is **`r round(coef(lm_robust(poverty ~ mc_state + mc_civ, data = Dat))["mc_state"], 2)`** — the very number from Lecture 9. One flat plane, one slope for all.
:::
:::

::: {.push-right}
```{r p2-add-plane, echo = FALSE, out.width='74%', fig.height = 5, fig.width = 5.5}
m2 <- lm(poverty ~ mc_state + mc_civ, data = Dat)
plane(m2, "mc_state", "mc_civ", Dat,
      xlab = "State own.", ylab = "Civ. lib.", zlab = "Poverty",
      theta = 45, phi = 15)
```
:::

::: {.notes}
Anchor on the continuity: the additive slope is −3.78, exactly Lecture 9's number, so nothing has changed yet. The plane is flat and tilted — as you slide along the civil-liberties axis, the tilt in the state-ownership direction never changes. That rigidity is precisely the assumption we are about to test. Keep the plane on screen; the next one bends.
:::

## Now let the plane bend

::: {.push-left}
```{r p2-int, results = 'hide'}
ols_int2 <- lm_robust(poverty ~ mc_state * mc_civ,
                      data = Dat)

modelsummary(
  list("Additive" = ols_add2, "Interaction" = ols_int2),
  coef_rename = c("mc_state" = "State ownership",
                  "mc_civ" = "Civil liberties",
                  "mc_state:mc_civ" = "State own. × Civ. lib."),
  stars = TRUE, gof_map = c("nobs", "r.squared"),
  output = "kableExtra"
)
```

::: {.content-box-red}
**An interaction implies non-linearity:** the surface is no longer flat. The fitted effect of state ownership now **twists** as civil liberties rise — though whether that twist is *real* is a question the table, not the plane, must answer.
:::
:::

::: {.push-right}
```{r p2-int-plane, echo = FALSE, out.width='74%', fig.height = 5, fig.width = 5.5}
m3 <- lm(poverty ~ mc_state * mc_civ, data = Dat)
plane(m3, "mc_state", "mc_civ", Dat,
      xlab = "State own.", ylab = "Civ. lib.", zlab = "Poverty",
      theta = 45, phi = 15)
```
:::

::: {.notes}
Here is the intuition the 3D view is for: adding the product warps the plane into a twisted surface — the slope in one direction genuinely changes as you move along the other. Say the slightly surprising thing out loud: an interaction between two continuous variables *is* a form of non-linearity, a saddle. The 3D plane is great for feeling that, and hopeless for reading exact numbers — which is why the next slide flattens it into lines.
:::

## Read it as lines, not a surface

::: {.push-left}
```{r p2-lines, echo = FALSE, out.width='96%', fig.height = 4.4, fig.width = 6}
b <- coef(ols_int2)
sd_c <- sd(Dat$mc_civ)
grid <- expand.grid(
  mc_state = seq(min(Dat$mc_state), max(Dat$mc_state), length.out = 50),
  mc_civ   = c(-sd_c, 0, sd_c)
)
grid$poverty <- predict(ols_int2, newdata = grid)
grid$Liberties <- factor(grid$mc_civ,
  labels = c("Low (autocratic)", "Average", "High (liberal)"))

ggplot(grid, aes(x = mc_state, y = poverty, color = Liberties)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#901A1E", "#b5892c", "#425570")) +
  labs(x = "State ownership (centred)", y = "Predicted % below $3.00 a day",
       color = "Civil liberties") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) + theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```
:::

::: {.push-right}
```{r p2-slopes, include = FALSE}
slope_lo <- b["mc_state"] + b["mc_state:mc_civ"] * (-sd_c)
slope_hi <- b["mc_state"] + b["mc_state:mc_civ"] * ( sd_c)
```

The same model, drawn as the state-ownership slope at **three** levels of civil liberties:

- **Low** civil liberties (autocratic): slope **`r round(slope_lo, 2)`** — state ownership *appears* to go with less poverty.
- **Average**: slope **`r round(b["mc_state"], 2)`**.
- **High** civil liberties (liberal): slope **`r round(slope_hi, 2)`** — state ownership *appears* to barely matter.

::: {.content-box-red}
**Beware — read the table first.** The interaction term is **not statistically significant** (`r round(coef(summary(ols_int2))["mc_state:mc_civ", "Pr(>|t|)"], 2)`). The fan of lines is *suggestive*, but we **cannot** conclude the slopes genuinely differ. A picture is not a p-value.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
The most important slide in Part 2, and the reason to teach it: the picture practically begs for a story — state ownership fights poverty under autocracy but not under liberal rule — and the story is **not supported by the data**, because the interaction term does not reach significance. Make them look at the table before the plot. This is the discipline the whole course is training: a suggestive fan of lines is a hypothesis, not a finding. The mechanics they just practised are correct; the substantive claim is one we are not entitled to make.
:::

## Two layers of humility {.inverse background-color="#901A1E"}

::: {.push-left}
::: {.content-box-red}
**First:** here the interaction was **not significant** — so we cannot even claim the slopes truly differ. The suggestive fan of lines may be noise.
:::
:::

::: {.push-right}
::: {.content-box-red}
**Second:** *even if it had been significant*, an interaction is still only a **description**. It says the associations depend on each other — never **which way the causation runs**.
:::
:::

::: {.lead .center}
For that we would need a clever causal research design — exactly the kind the rest of the course is about.
:::

::: {.notes}
Close Part 2 with the two separate cautions stacked, because students conflate them. First: this particular interaction is not significant, so the pattern is not established at all. Second, and more general: a *significant* interaction would still be only a richer description — it tells you the slopes depend on each other, not why. Neither the picture nor a small p-value buys you causation. That double humility is the bridge back to the design-based half of the course.
:::

## Today's general lessons {.inverse background-color="#901A1E"}

1. Two predictors **interact** when the effect of one **depends on the value of the other**. You specify it by adding their **product** — `y ~ x1 * x2`.

2. With an interaction, each **main term is conditional** — the effect of one variable *where the other is zero*. **Mean-centre** continuous predictors so "zero" is the average and the main terms are readable.

3. The **interaction term** has two equivalent readings: how one slope changes with the other variable, and vice versa.

4. Between two continuous variables an interaction is a form of **non-linearity** — a twisted surface. Read it as **predicted lines at a few values** of the moderator, not off the 3D plane.

5. Always **check the interaction's significance before telling its story** — a suggestive fan of lines is not a finding. And even a *significant* interaction is still only a **description**: it says the slopes depend on each other, not which way the **causation** runs.

::: {.notes}
Run through fast. If one lesson survives, make it the pairing of two and five: mean-centre so your numbers mean something, and never let a fancy interaction fool you into thinking you have earned a causal claim. Everything today enriched the description; none of it identified an effect.
:::

## Check yourself: today's goals

::: {.checklist}
- Given a scatter with two visibly different slopes, write the model that captures it and say which term is the interaction.
- Explain what the "main term" coefficients mean in an interaction model — and why you would mean-centre first.
- Read an interaction as predicted lines, and state the effect of one predictor at low, average and high values of the other.
:::

::: {.content-box-green}
Shaky on any of these? That is what this week's **Absalon quiz** and the **Friday exercise class** are for.
:::

## Today's important functions

::: {.small}
- `lm_robust(y ~ x1 * x2, ...)`: fit an interaction — `*` adds both main terms **and** their product.
- `lm_robust(y ~ x1 * x2 + c, ...)`: an interaction **plus** an additive control.
- `mutate(mc_x = x - mean(x))`: mean-centre, so a main term reads at the average.
- `predict(model, newdata = ...)`: predicted values on a grid — the basis for the predicted-line plot.
- `factor(..., labels = ...)`: turn low / average / high moderator values into readable line labels.
:::

## References

::: {.small}
```{r ref, results = 'asis', echo = FALSE}
PrintBibliography(myBib)
```
:::

```{=html}
<script>
(function () {
  function fmt(s) { var m = Math.floor(s / 60), ss = s % 60; return m + ":" + (ss < 10 ? "0" : "") + ss; }
  function build(el) {
    var total = (parseInt(el.getAttribute("data-min"), 10) || 5) * 60, rem = total, id = null;
    el.innerHTML =
      '<div class="kt-display">' + fmt(rem) + '</div>' +
      '<div class="kt-btns">' +
        '<button class="kt-start" type="button">Start</button>' +
        '<button class="kt-pause" type="button">Pause</button>' +
        '<button class="kt-reset" type="button">Reset</button>' +
      '</div>';
    var disp = el.querySelector(".kt-display");
    function render() { disp.textContent = fmt(rem); el.classList.toggle("kt-done", rem <= 0); }
    function start() { if (id) return; id = setInterval(function () { if (rem > 0) { rem--; render(); } else { stop(); } }, 1000); }
    function stop() { clearInterval(id); id = null; }
    function reset() { stop(); rem = total; render(); }
    el.querySelector(".kt-start").onclick = start;
    el.querySelector(".kt-pause").onclick = stop;
    el.querySelector(".kt-reset").onclick = reset;
    el._start = start; el._reset = reset; render();
  }
  function init() {
    document.querySelectorAll(".ku-timer").forEach(build);
    if (window.Reveal && Reveal.on) {
      Reveal.on("slidechanged", function (e) {
        document.querySelectorAll(".ku-timer").forEach(function (t) { if (t._reset) t._reset(); });
        var here = e.currentSlide ? e.currentSlide.querySelectorAll(".ku-timer") : [];
        here.forEach(function (t) { if (t._start) setTimeout(t._start, 250); });
      });
    }
  }
  if (document.readyState !== "loading") init();
  else document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", init);
})();
</script>
```
